scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a CPG-specific handoff package with explicit ownership, lot-aware artifacts, and blocker state.
Missing genealogy, release, and destination-acceptance prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than a routable handoff.
Duplicate ownership and conflicting schedules were escalated with an owner map instead of dropped into generic coordination.
Conflicting ERP, MES, WMS, and QMS lot records were preserved as a traceability blocker and not collapsed into a confident answer.
Quality hold posture was preserved and formal release authority stayed with quality owners.
Supplier substitution approval was rejected and routed to supplier-quality and change-control owners.
The lane refused to absorb production, release, and transport execution work and returned boundary-safe routing guidance.
Missing tenant authority and handoff maps correctly triggered a low-confidence blocked-dependency escalation.
Conflicting public and tenant hold-handling rules were escalated for human resolution instead of synthesized into a definitive instruction.
Industry-specific CPG terms were normalized into authoritative artifact and handoff states before routing.
Closure was only confirmed after destination-owner proof and required artifact completeness were explicit.
Supplier nonconformance was escalated with throughput context preserved and without unsupported continuation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to CPG supply coordination, traceability, hold boundaries, and owner routing.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the issue contract.
